title: Basic Principles of PT: Capillary Action
narrationScript: You have used capillary action your whole life without naming it. Touch the corner of a paper towel to spilled coffee and the liquid climbs into the towel, uphill, with no pump pushing it. The wick of an oil lamp lifts fuel to the flame the same way. Penetrant testing simply hires that force twice. Once to carry penetrant down into a flaw, and once to pull it back out into the developer where you can see it. Understand this one mechanism and the whole method makes sense, every dwell time, every temperature limit, every cleaning rule. Start inside the liquid. The molecules of a liquid attract one another. That mutual attraction is called cohesion, and at the surface it makes the liquid behave as if it were wrapped in a thin stretched skin. We call that effect surface tension. It is why a water drop pulls itself into a bead, and why a carefully placed needle can rest on a still glass of water. There is a second attraction in play, the pull between the liquid and any solid it touches. That one is called adhesion. The contest between those two forces decides everything. When adhesion to the solid wins, the liquid spreads out and clings. We say it wets the surface. When cohesion wins, the liquid gathers into a bead and stands off the surface. We measure that contest with the contact angle. Set a drop on a clean horizontal plate and measure the angle between the solid surface and the edge of the drop, taken inside the liquid. An angle below ninety degrees means the liquid wets. An angle above ninety degrees means it does not. Water on a freshly waxed hood beads up near or above ninety. A good penetrant on clean metal spreads almost flat, with a contact angle of only a few degrees. Wetting is the gatekeeper. A liquid that will not wet the surface will never enter a flaw, no matter how long you wait. Now the pump itself. Stand a fine glass tube in a wetting liquid and the liquid climbs the bore and holds there, above the level outside the tube. The climb is driven by surface tension acting around the curved liquid surface inside the tube, the meniscus. Here is the part to burn into memory. The narrower the tube, the higher the climb. The pull grows as the passage shrinks. A crack is just that tube flattened into a slot, two rough walls a hair apart, and the same rule applies. The tighter the crack, the harder it pulls penetrant in. The flaws you most need to find, the tight ones, are exactly the ones that grab penetrant most strongly. And because capillary forces completely dwarf gravity at that tiny scale, position does not matter. Penetrant enters a crack on a vertical wall or an overhead surface just as well as on a bench top. A real crack is a dead end, though, not an open pipe. The air inside has to go somewhere. Because a crack is open along its whole length at the surface, air escapes through sections the penetrant has not covered yet while liquid advances elsewhere. The fill happens, but it is not instant, and the tightest flaws fill slowest. That is the entire reason dwell time exists. Typical procedures call for a penetrant dwell of at least ten minutes, and longer when you are hunting tight fatigue cracks or working near the cold end of the temperature range. Your written procedure governs. One rule inside that window. Keep the surface wet. If penetrant dries to a film during dwell, it stops feeding the flaw and it will not remove or develop properly. Reapply it, or clean the part and start over. What about viscosity, the liquid's resistance to flow? Viscosity sets how fast penetrant moves, not whether it can enter. A thick penetrant on a cold morning still wets and still penetrates. It just needs more time. That is why the standard temperature band in many procedures runs from about forty degrees to one hundred twenty five degrees Fahrenheit. Below the band the liquid thickens and dwell stretches out. Above it, the penetrant can dry on the part and its dyes can degrade. Last comes the second hire. After you remove the surface excess, the flaw stays charged with penetrant, held in place by the same capillary grip that pulled it in. Developer is a layer of fine white powder, and to the trapped liquid it looks like thousands of fresh capillary passages pressed against the flaw mouth. It works like a blotter. Penetrant wicks up into the powder and spreads sideways, painting a mark many times wider than the crack that fed it. Give it time, typically at least ten minutes of development, and read the surface within your procedure's window, because an overdeveloped indication bleeds wide and fuzzy. Two questions run this whole method. Can the penetrant get in? That is wetting, openness, and cleanliness. And did you give it enough time? That is viscosity, temperature, and dwell.
imagePrompt: Extreme macro of glossy red dye penetrant creeping along a hairline fatigue crack in brushed stainless steel, the droplet edge spreading nearly flat across the machined surface under warm shop lighting.

sections.heading: A Force You Already Know
sections.body:
Touch the corner of a paper towel to spilled coffee and the liquid climbs into it - uphill, with no pump. A lamp wick lifts oil to the flame the same way, and a sugar cube dipped edge-first into tea darkens to the top. All of these are **capillary action**: a liquid drawn spontaneously into fine passages by forces at its own surface.

Penetrant testing hires that force **twice**. During dwell, capillary action carries penetrant *into* any discontinuity open to the surface. During development, the same force pulls the trapped penetrant *back out* into a blotter-like powder coating, where it spreads into a visible indication. Every practical rule in this method - dwell times, temperature limits, the obsession with pre-cleaning, the choice of developer - exists to serve or protect that one mechanism. Understand it once, properly, and the rest of the course is applications.

sections.heading: Surface Tension, Adhesion, and Wetting
sections.body:
Liquid molecules attract one another - that mutual pull is **cohesion**, and at the liquid's surface it produces **surface tension**, making the surface behave like a thin stretched membrane. It is why a free drop pulls itself into a sphere and why a carefully placed needle floats on still water.

A second force competes: **adhesion**, the attraction between the liquid and a solid it touches. The contest decides whether the liquid **wets**. If adhesion wins, the liquid spreads and clings; if cohesion wins, it beads up and stands off the surface.

The scorecard is the **contact angle** - the angle between the solid surface and the drop's edge, measured *inside* the liquid. Below 90° the liquid wets and will enter fine openings; above 90° it does not. Water on a waxed panel sits near or above 90°. A well-formulated penetrant on clean metal spreads almost flat, with a contact angle of **only a few degrees** - nearly perfect wetting.

This is the gatekeeper property: a liquid that cannot wet the surface will never enter a flaw, no matter how long it sits. And wetting is fragile - an oily film, fingerprints, or residues on the part raise the effective contact angle and choke off entry, which is why pre-cleaning is half the method.

sections.caption: Wetting versus non-wetting: a penetrant spreads to a contact angle of a few degrees, while a non-wetting liquid beads above ninety degrees and cannot enter fine openings.

sections.heading: The Capillary Pump
sections.body:
Stand a fine glass tube in a wetting liquid and the liquid climbs the bore, held above the outside level by surface tension acting around the curved **meniscus**. The rule that matters: **the narrower the passage, the stronger the pull and the higher the climb**. The capillary driving pressure rises in proportion to surface tension times the cosine of the contact angle, divided by the size of the gap - shrink the gap and the pull grows.

A crack is that tube flattened into a slot: two rough walls a hair apart. The same physics applies, so **the tightest cracks pull penetrant hardest** - the flaws most invisible to the eye grab penetrant most strongly. And because capillary pressure dwarfs gravity at flaw scale, **part orientation is irrelevant**: penetrant enters cracks on vertical and overhead surfaces just as readily as on a bench.

One complication: a crack is a *dead-end* capillary, and the air inside must go somewhere. Because the crack is open along its whole surface length, air escapes through sections the liquid has not yet covered while penetrant advances elsewhere. Filling happens - but not instantly, and the tightest flaws fill slowest. That single fact is the physical justification for dwell time.

sections.caption: Capillary rise grows as the passage narrows - and a crack behaves as a flattened capillary, so tighter flaws pull penetrant harder.

sections.heading: Time, Viscosity, and Temperature
sections.body:
**Viscosity** is a liquid's resistance to flow - and its role is widely misunderstood. Viscosity governs **how fast** penetrant moves, *not whether* it can enter. A thick penetrant on a cold morning still wets and still penetrates; it simply needs more time. Surface tension and contact angle decide ability; viscosity and temperature decide speed.

That is the logic behind two numbers you will live with:

- **Penetrant dwell**: typical procedures require **at least 10 minutes**, with longer dwells for tight fatigue cracks, stress-corrosion cracking, or parts near the cold end of the range. Your written procedure governs.
- **Temperature band**: standard practice commonly allows about **40-125 °F (4-52 °C)** for both part and penetrant. Below the band, the penetrant thickens and dwell stretches; above it, the penetrant can dry on the part and its dyes can degrade. Outside the band, the procedure must be qualified for those conditions.

One discipline inside the dwell window: **keep the surface wet**. Penetrant that dries to a film stops feeding the flaw and will neither remove nor develop properly - reapply penetrant or reclean and restart. And remember that residues matter even when invisible: acids and chromates left in a flaw can destroy a fluorescent dye's glow before you ever get to the booth.

sections.title: Ability versus speed
sections.body: **Wetting (contact angle), an open flaw, and a clean cavity decide WHETHER penetrant enters. Viscosity and temperature decide HOW FAST. Dwell time buys the time the physics needs.** When a tight crack is missed, the failure traces back to one of those two questions - the penetrant could not get in, or it was not given the chance.

sections.heading: Reversing the Flow: Bleed-Out
sections.body:
After dwell, the surface excess is removed - but the flaw stays charged, because the same capillary grip that pulled penetrant in now holds it against everything short of an over-aggressive wash. The part may look perfectly clean while every tight crack in it is still loaded with dye.

**Developer** turns that hidden charge into something you can see. A developer coat is a layer of finely divided white powder, and to the trapped liquid it presents thousands of fresh capillary passages pressed directly against the flaw mouth. It is a blotter, working capillary action in reverse: penetrant wicks out of the flaw into the powder and spreads laterally, painting a mark **many times wider than the opening that fed it**. That spreading is the method's amplification - the reason a crack a fraction of a thousandth of an inch wide becomes a bold line - and the white coat adds contrast behind a red indication or a non-fluorescing background behind a glowing one.

Development takes time: commonly **at least 10 minutes** before interpretation, reading the part within the procedure's window, because over-development keeps bleeding until indications grow wide and fuzzy. The bleed-out itself carries information - a fast, spreading return from a wiped indication signals a large hidden volume - and you will use that in interpretation lessons later.

sections.caption: One force, two directions: capillary action fills the flaw during dwell, holds the charge through removal, and blots penetrant back out into the developer as a widened indication.

figures.title: Capillary rise: tight paths pull harder

interactive.title: What Controls Penetrant Entry?
interactive.intro: Classify each factor by the role it actually plays when penetrant meets a surface-breaking flaw.
buckets: Little or no effect on entry

items.label: Contact angle (wetting ability)
items.why: A liquid that does not wet - contact angle near or above ninety degrees - never enters the flaw at all; good penetrants wet at only a few degrees.

items.label: Flaw opening clear at the surface
items.why: Capillary entry needs an open path; metal smeared over the mouth, or a flaw that is fully subsurface, means no entry, period.

items.label: Oil, paint, or water already inside the flaw
items.why: A contaminated cavity is already full - penetrant cannot displace it, so entry is blocked no matter how long the dwell.

items.label: Penetrant viscosity
items.why: Viscosity is resistance to flow: a thicker penetrant still wets and still enters, it just needs a longer dwell to fill tight flaws.

items.label: Length of the penetrant dwell
items.why: Dwell buys the time capillary filling needs - it compensates for slow flow, though it can never create wetting that is not there.

items.label: Part temperature within the standard band
items.why: Cold penetrant is thicker and fills slower, which is why procedures extend dwell near the cold end of the roughly 40-125 °F band.

items.label: Part orientation (vertical or overhead)
items.why: At flaw scale, capillary pressure dwarfs gravity - penetrant enters cracks on vertical and overhead surfaces just as well as on a bench.

items.label: Dye color or fluorescence of the penetrant
items.why: The dye sets how visible the indication will be later; it has essentially nothing to do with whether or how fast the liquid enters the flaw.

checkQuestions.question: What actually drives penetrant into a tight surface crack?
options: Gravity pulling the liquid down into the opening
Surface tension acting with strong wetting - a contact angle far below ninety degrees
Spray pressure from the aerosol or applicator
A chemical attraction between the dye and flaw surfaces
checkQuestions.explanation: Capillary entry is driven by surface tension combined with the penetrant's ability to wet the part; applied pressure and gravity are negligible at flaw scale, which is also why orientation does not matter.

checkQuestions.question: A tight fatigue crack and a broad, shallow gouge sit side by side on the same part. Which statement is correct?
options: The gouge develops the stronger capillary pull because it holds more liquid
Both features experience the same capillary pressure
The crack develops the stronger capillary pressure, because the pull grows as the gap narrows
Neither fills unless the surface is horizontal
checkQuestions.explanation: Capillary driving pressure increases as the passage gets smaller, so the tight crack pulls penetrant in harder than the open gouge - the flaws hardest to see grab penetrant most strongly.

checkQuestions.question: Your unheated shop is at about forty-five degrees Fahrenheit - inside the standard band, but cold. What does the cold mainly change?
options: The penetrant can no longer wet the surface at all
Capillary action reverses direction below fifty degrees
Dwell can be shortened because cold liquids cling better
Entry slows because the penetrant is more viscous, so the procedure typically requires a longer dwell
checkQuestions.explanation: Temperature works through viscosity: cold thickens the penetrant and slows filling without removing its ability to enter, so procedures compensate with extended dwell near the cold end of the band.

checkQuestions.question: Why does the developed indication appear many times wider than the crack that produced it?
options: The developer's fine powder presents countless new capillary paths that blot penetrant out of the flaw and spread it laterally
The crack physically widens as the developer cures
Ultraviolet light optically enlarges the stain
The penetrant expands as its solvent flashes off
checkQuestions.explanation: Development is capillary action in reverse: the powder wicks penetrant out of the flaw and spreads it sideways, so the visible mark is far wider than the opening - that spreading is the method's amplification.

checkQuestions.question: Halfway through a long dwell on a hot, breezy day, the penetrant coat has dried to a tacky film. Why is that a problem?
options: Dried penetrant develops excessive capillary force and over-penetrates
It only matters for visible dye penetrants, not fluorescent ones
A dried film stops feeding the flaw and will not remove or bleed out properly - rewet it, or reclean and restart per the procedure
Drying improves sensitivity but permanently stains the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Penetrant must stay liquid to keep filling the flaw and later to wash off and bleed back out; once dried it does none of those, so the fix is to keep the surface wet during dwell or start the process over.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §1.3 (basic principles of PT)
Practices such as ASTM E165 and ASTM E1417 (dwell and temperature practice context)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (penetrant examination, general practice context)
General capillarity physics: surface tension, contact angle, capillary rise (reference principles)
